questions.question: Let I_A be the indicator of event A (I_A = 1 if A occurs, else 0). If P(A) = 0.2, what is E[I_A]?

explanations.D: Correct. E[I_A] = 1·P(A) + 0·P(Aᶜ) = P(A) = 0.2.
explanations.A: This is P(Aᶜ) = 1 − P(A), the probability the event does NOT occur.
explanations.B: This squares P(A); since I_A² = I_A, the expectation stays P(A), not P(A)².
explanations.C: Halving P(A) has no basis; the indicator expectation equals P(A) exactly.

questions.question: You roll a fair die 2 times. Let N be the number of sixes. Using indicators, what is E[N]?

explanations.B: Correct. Write N = I₁ + … + Iₙ with each E[Iⱼ] = 1/6, so E[N] = 2·(1/6) = 1/3.

questions.question: You flip a coin with P(heads) = 1/2 until the first heads, counting the successful flip. Let T be the number of flips. What is E[T]?

explanations.C: Correct. T is Geometric(p = 1/2), so E[T] = 1/p = 2.
explanations.A: This is p = 1/2 itself, not the expected waiting time 1/p.
explanations.B: This is (1−p)/p, the expected number of FAILURES before the first success, not the total flips.
explanations.D: This doubles the correct mean 1/p; the geometric mean is exactly 1/p.

questions.question: Let I be a Bernoulli random variable with P(I=1) = 0.2. What is Var(I)?

explanations.A: This is the mean E[I] = p, not the variance.
explanations.C: This is p² = E[I]²; variance is E[I²] − E[I]² = p − p² = p(1−p).
explanations.D: This is P(I=0) = 1 − p, not the variance.

questions.question: X equals 0 with probability 0.5 and 10 with probability 0.5. What is Var(X)?

explanations.B: Correct. E[X] = 5 and E[X²] = 0²·0.5 + 10²·0.5 = 50, so Var(X) = E[X²] − E[X]² = 50 − 25 = 25.
explanations.A: This is the mean E[X], not the variance.
explanations.C: This is the squared gap (a−b)² without the p(1−p) weighting.
explanations.D: This is E[X²]; the variance subtracts E[X]² from it.

questions.question: X equals 2 with probability 0.5 and 8 with probability 0.5. What is Var(X)?

explanations.A: This is the mean E[X], not the variance.
explanations.B: This is the standard deviation √Var(X), not the variance itself.
explanations.D: This is the squared gap (a−b)² without the p(1−p) weighting.

questions.question: A uniformly random permutation of {1, …, 5} is drawn. Let F be the number of fixed points (i where the value equals position i). What is E[F]?

explanations.B: Correct. F = ΣIⱼ with E[Iⱼ] = P(position j fixed) = 1/5; summing over 5 positions gives 5·(1/5) = 1.
explanations.A: This would require every position to be a fixed point with certainty.
explanations.C: This is the probability a single position is fixed, not the expected total count.
explanations.D: There is no halving here; the indicator sum gives exactly 1 for every n ≥ 1.

questions.question: 6 guests check identical-looking hats; the hats are returned in a uniformly random order. What is the expected number of guests who get their own hat back?

explanations.C: Some guest can match, so the expectation is positive, not 0.

questions.question: Let X be a random variable with E[X] = 3.5, and let Y = X (perfectly dependent). What is E[X + Y]?

explanations.D: Correct. E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y] = 3.5 + 3.5 = 7; linearity holds even though X and Y are perfectly dependent.
explanations.A: This ignores the second copy of X; linearity still adds both expectations.
explanations.B: This multiplies; E of a sum is a sum of expectations, not a product.
explanations.C: X + Y = 2X here, which has positive expectation, not 0.

questions.question: Which of the following is a discrete random variable?
options: The exact height of a randomly chosen adult
The exact time in seconds until the next bus
The exact temperature at noon tomorrow
The number of heads in 5 coin flips

explanations.D: Correct. It takes integer values 0 through 5, each with a probability — the hallmark of a discrete random variable.
explanations.A: Height to arbitrary precision is modeled as a continuous random variable.
explanations.B: Exact continuous time takes uncountably many values, so it is continuous.
explanations.C: A real-valued measurement like temperature is continuous, not discrete.

questions.question: A fair die is rolled and X is the face shown. Which statement is TRUE?
options: X is continuous on the interval [1,6]
X is not random because the die is fair

explanations.A: Correct. X takes finitely many values each with probability 1/6, so it is discrete.
explanations.B: X only takes the six integer faces, not every real number in [1,6].
explanations.C: 3.5 is E[X]; no single roll ever shows 3.5.
explanations.D: Fairness means equal probabilities, not a fixed outcome — X still varies.

questions.question: Which identity holds for ALL random variables X and Y, even when they are dependent?

explanations.B: Correct. Linearity of expectation never requires independence.
explanations.A: This requires independence (or zero covariance); it can fail for dependent variables.
explanations.C: This needs zero covariance; it fails for dependent variables in general.
explanations.D: Expectation does not pass through division; this is false in general.

questions.question: Which list of probabilities is a valid PMF for a variable taking values 0, 1, 2?

explanations.C: Correct. All values are in [0,1] and they sum to exactly 1.
explanations.A: These sum to 1.1, violating the total-probability-equals-1 rule.
explanations.B: A negative probability is impossible.
explanations.D: These sum to 1.5, which is not a valid PMF.

questions.question: X takes values 1 and 2 with P(X=1) = p. For a valid PMF, P(X=2) must equal:

explanations.D: Correct. The two probabilities must sum to 1, so P(X=2) = 1 − p.
explanations.A: This forces P(X=1)+P(X=2)=2p, which equals 1 only if p=0.5.
explanations.B: This exceeds 1 for any p > 0, impossible for a probability.
explanations.C: This does not follow from the requirement that probabilities sum to 1.

questions.question: For an indicator variable I_A, which statement is correct?

explanations.A: Correct. Since I_A is 0 or 1, squaring leaves it unchanged, so E[I_A²] = E[I_A] = P(A).
explanations.B: Squaring P(A) is wrong; E[I_A] = P(A) directly.
explanations.C: 1 − P(A) is the probability A does not occur, i.e. E of the complement indicator.
explanations.D: By definition an indicator is only ever 0 or 1.

questions.question: Which operation does NOT generally satisfy a simple product/sum rule without independence?
options: E[X + c] for a constant c

explanations.B: Correct. E[XY] = E[X]E[Y] requires independence; it can fail otherwise.
explanations.A: This always linearizes, regardless of dependence.
explanations.C: E[X + c] = E[X] + c always holds.
explanations.D: E[−X] = −E[X] always holds.

questions.question: For a Geometric(p) variable counting trials until the first success, E equals:

explanations.C: Correct. The expected waiting time is the reciprocal of the success probability.
explanations.A: p is the per-trial success probability, not the mean waiting time.
explanations.B: 1 − p is the per-trial failure probability.
explanations.D: p² has no role as the mean of a geometric variable.
